article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Perceived Stress, Time: F(2, 112) = 35.75, p < .0001, partial η² = 0.50, 95% CI [0.36, 0.60]
textual_interpretation: Significant main effect of time on perceived stress
notes: Table 3, Mixed ANOVA 2(Group: MBCT vs control) × 3(Time: pre, post, follow-up), N=60 (30+30), partial η²=0.50 large effect

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Perceived Stress, Group: F(1, 56) = 28.42, p < .0001, partial η² = 0.48, 95% CI [0.31, 0.58]
textual_interpretation: Significant main effect of group on perceived stress — MBCT group showed greater reductions
notes: Table 3, Mixed ANOVA, partial η²=0.48 large effect

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Perceived Stress, Time × Group: F(2, 112) = 59.96, p < .0001, partial η² = 0.63, 95% CI [0.51, 0.70]
textual_interpretation: Highly significant time × group interaction for perceived stress — MBCT led to significant and sustained improvements while control group showed no meaningful change
notes: Table 3, Mixed ANOVA, partial η²=0.63 large effect

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Mental Health (PCS), Time: F(2, 112) = 20.38, p < .0001, partial η² = 0.37, 95% CI [0.22, 0.49]
textual_interpretation: Significant main effect of time on psychosomatic symptoms (PCS)
notes: Table 3, Mixed ANOVA, partial η²=0.37 large effect

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Mental Health (PCS), Group: F(1, 56) = 16.47, p < .0001, partial η² = 0.32, 95% CI [0.16, 0.45]
textual_interpretation: Significant main effect of group on psychosomatic symptoms — MBCT group showed greater reductions
notes: Table 3, Mixed ANOVA, partial η²=0.32 large effect

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Mental Health (PCS), Time × Group: F(2, 112) = 32.05, p < .0001, partial η² = 0.48, 95% CI [0.33, 0.58]
textual_interpretation: Significant time × group interaction for psychosomatic symptoms — MBCT group improved while control remained unchanged
notes: Table 3, Mixed ANOVA, partial η²=0.48 large effect

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Perceived Stress, Pre-test vs. Post-test: Mean Difference = 2.91, p = .001, 95% CI [1.88, 3.94]
textual_interpretation: Perceived stress significantly decreased between pre-test and post-test (Bonferroni-adjusted)
notes: Table 4, Bonferroni-adjusted pairwise comparison. Test statistic not reported explicitly

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Perceived Stress, Pre-test vs. Follow-up: Mean Difference = 2.60, p = .001, 95% CI [1.55, 3.65]
textual_interpretation: Perceived stress significantly decreased between pre-test and follow-up — sustained stress reduction

article_title: Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy for perceived stress and psychosomatic symptoms in Chinese adolescent girls: a mixed-methods school-based study of parent and teacher perceptions
raw_text: Perceived Stress, Post-test vs. Follow-up: Mean Difference = −0.31, p = .21
textual_interpretation: No significant change in perceived stress between post-test and follow-up — indicating sustained effect

textual_interpretation: Significant improvement in psychosomatic symptoms from pre-test to post-test
